summary: DHH responds to Mena Trott of Six Apart's defense of building big companies, arguing that the internet age has made it possible for small teams to accomplish extraordinary things. He rejects the framing that entrepreneurs must choose between a lifestyle business and a VC-funded large operation. While acknowledging Mena's right to choose scale, he insists that bigness is now optional, not required, for making a significant impact.
key_points: The internet, cheap infrastructure, and productive development tools have given small teams unprecedented power to build big things
The false dichotomy between 'lifestyle business' and 'VC-powered large team' is frustrating and misleading
Being big to do big is now optional, not necessary — Six Apart chose scale but didn't have to
Entrepreneurs and customers alike should reject the assumption that size equals impact
money_quotes: Never before in the history of the world has the potential to do extraordinary things been this big for the smallest of teams.

The reach of the net, the low/no-cost of infrastructure, and rise of fiercely-productive environments have empowered those with passion near superhuman strength in business, by last-century standards.
It's no longer necessary to be big to do big. It's optional.
Don't buy it if you're a customer. Don't buy it if you're an entrepreneur in the making.
Mena and crew chose to do so, but they had every opportunity to have done it differently.
key_insight: The internet economy has made scale optional — small teams can now achieve outsized impact without VC funding or large headcounts.
